title: REST API Intro
content:
REST API Introduction

What is REST?

REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for providing standards between computer systems on the web, making it easier for systems to communicate with each other.

  • Stateless: Each request from client to server must contain all the information to understand the request.
  • Client-Server: The client and server are independent of each other.
  • Cacheable: Responses must define themselves as cacheable or not.
  • Uniform Interface: Simplifies and decouples the architecture.
  • Layered System: A client cannot ordinarily tell whether it is connected directly to the end server or to an intermediary.

title: HTTP Methods
content:
HTTP Methods

Common HTTP Verbs

REST APIs use standard HTTP methods to perform actions on resources.

  • GET: Retrieve a representation of a resource.
  • POST: Create a new resource.
  • PUT: Update an existing resource (full replacement).
  • PATCH: Update a part of an existing resource.
  • DELETE: Remove a resource.
Example GET Request
GET /api/users/123 HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

title: Status Codes
content:
HTTP Status Codes

Response Categories

  • 2xx (Success): 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content
  • 3xx (Redirection): 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified
  • 4xx (Client Error): 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found
  • 5xx (Server Error): 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable

title: Resource Naming
content:
Resource Naming

Best Practices for URIs

URIs should be intuitive and use nouns instead of verbs.

  • Use Plural Nouns: Prefer /users over /user.
  • Avoid Verbs: Use GET /cars instead of GET /getAllCars.
  • Hyphens over Underscores: Use /user-profiles for better readability.
  • Lowercase: URIs are case-sensitive (except for scheme and host), so use lowercase.
Resource URI Example
GET /api/v1/customers/550/orders

title: HTTP Headers
content:
HTTP Headers

Metadata for Requests/Responses

Headers pass additional information between the client and the server.

  • Content-Type: Indicates the media type of the resource (e.g., application/json).
  • Accept: Tells the server what media types the client can handle.
  • Authorization: Contains credentials for authenticating a client with a server.
  • User-Agent: Identifies the client software.
Request with Headers
GET /api/items HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer my-token

title: Query Parameters
content:
Query Parameters

Filtering and Searching

Query parameters are appended to the end of a URL after a ?.

Query Parameter Example
GET /api/products?category=electronics&min_price=100

Multiple parameters are separated by &.

title: Path Parameters
content:
Path Parameters

Identifying Specific Resources

Path parameters are part of the URI path and identify a specific resource.

Path Parameter Example
GET /api/users/42
DELETE /api/posts/1024

In this case, 42 and 1024 are the parameters.

title: Request & Response Body
content:
Request & Response Body

Data Exchange Format

The body contains the actual data being sent or received, usually in JSON format.

POST Request Body (JSON)
{
  "username": "alice",
  "email": "alice@example.com"
}

title: Authentication Basics
content:
Authentication

Securing Your API

  • Basic Auth: Uses a base64 encoded username:password string in the header.
  • API Keys: A unique string passed in the header or query string.
API Key Example
GET /api/data?api_key=your_secret_key_here

title: OAuth2 & JWT
content:
OAuth2 & JWT

Modern Authentication

OAuth2 is a framework for authorization, while JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact way to transmit information as a JSON object.

  • Bearer Token: Tokens are usually sent in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header.
  • Stateful vs Stateless: JWTs allow for stateless authentication.

title: Pagination
content:
API Pagination

Handling Large Datasets

When an API returns a lot of items, pagination is used to split the data into chunks.

Limit/Offset Pagination
GET /api/products?limit=20&offset=40

title: Filtering & Sorting
content:
Filtering & Sorting

Refining Results

Allow clients to filter and sort the data they receive.

Filter & Sort Example
GET /api/users?status=active&sort=created_at,desc

title: API Versioning
content:
API Versioning

Managing Changes

Versioning allows you to make breaking changes without breaking existing clients.

  • URI Versioning: /api/v1/products
  • Header Versioning: Accept: application/vnd.example.v2+json

title: Error Handling
content:
Error Handling Best Practices

Consistent Error Responses

Always return meaningful error messages and correct status codes.

Error Response Example
{
  "error": "validation_failed",
  "message": "The email field is required.",
  "code": 400
}

title: HATEOAS
content:
HATEOAS

Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State

A REST principle where the server returns links to other related resources.

HATEOAS Example
{
  "id": "123",
  "links": [
    { "rel": "self", "href": "/api/users/123" },
    { "rel": "orders", "href": "/api/users/123/orders" }
  ]
}

title: API Documentation
content:
API Documentation

Swagger & OpenAPI

Standard ways to describe and document REST APIs so others can easily use them.

  • Interactive Maps: Tools like Swagger UI allow testing APIs directly from docs.
  • Specification: OpenAPI is the industry standard specification.

title: Course Summary
content:
REST API Mastery 🏆

You're an API Expert!

You have completed the full REST API course! You now have a deep understanding of REST architecture, from basic methods to advanced concepts like HATEOAS and security.

  • Fundamentals: Protocol basics, Methods, and Status Codes.
  • Architecture: Resource naming, Versioning, and HATEOAS.
  • Production Ready: Authentication, Documentation, and Error handling.

Go forth and build amazing, scalable APIs!
